title: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System
authors: Mary Jane Lim-Fat, Patrick Y. Wen

items.subtopic: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.title: Understanding Headache Patterns in Brain Tumor Patients
items.content: Headaches associated with brain tumors often present as holopnephalic, but can also be ipsilateral to the tumor. While a classic pattern is morning predominance improving during the day, this occurs only in a minority of patients.
keyPoints: Holopnephalic headaches are common in brain tumor patients.
Headaches may improve as the day progresses, though this pattern is seen in a minority.
Ipsilateral headaches can occur relative to the tumor location.

items.subtopic: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.title: Recognizing Seizure Presentation in Brain Tumor Patients
items.content: Seizures are a significant clinical feature in brain tumors, occurring in ~25% of patients with brain metastases or malignant gliomas. They can be the initial symptom in up to 90% of low-grade glioma cases.
keyPoints: Seizures occur in approximately 25% of patients with brain metastases or malignant gliomas.
Up to 90% of low-grade glioma patients may present initially with seizures.
All seizures from a brain tumor will have a focal onset.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Preferred Imaging Modality for Suspected Brain Tumor
items.content: Cranial MRI with gadolinium contrast is the preferred diagnostic test for suspected brain tumors. CT scans are reserved for patients who cannot undergo MRI.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium contrast is preferred for diagnosing brain tumors.
CT scan is an alternative for those unable to have MRI.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROIMAGING | Brain Tumors

items.title: Characteristics of Malignant and Low-Grade Gliomas on Imaging
items.content: Malignant brain tumors enhance with gadolinium, show central necrosis, and are surrounded by edema. Low-grade gliomas do not enhance and are best seen on FLAIR MRI sequences.
keyPoints: Malignant tumors: Enhance with gadolinium, central necrosis, surrounding edema.
Low-grade gliomas: Do not enhance; visible on FLAIR sequences.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROIMAGING | Brain Tumors

items.title: Role of Functional MRI and PET in Presurgical Planning
items.content: Functional MRI helps define eloquent cortex areas, while PET assesses metabolic activity. These techniques distinguish tumor progression from treatment-induced necrosis.
keyPoints: Functional MRI: Identifies sensory, motor, language cortex for surgery planning.
PET: Assesses lesion metabolic activity; distinguishes progression from necrosis.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROIMAGING | Brain Tumors

items.title: Risk Factors and Molecular Pathogenesis of Diffuse Gliomas
items.content: Diffuse gliomas are the most common malignant primary brain tumors, with no identified etiology for most cases. Established risk factors include exposure to ionizing radiation and immunosuppression. The molecular pathogenesis involves genetic alterations such as loss of tumor-suppressor genes (e.g., p53, CDKN2A/B, PTEN) and amplification of protooncogenes like EGFR and PDGFR.
keyPoints: Most primary brain tumors have no identified etiology.
Established risk factors: ionizing radiation and immunosuppression.
No proven association with electromagnetic fields, head injury, N-nitroso compounds, or occupational risks.
Genetic alterations include loss of tumor-suppressor genes (p53, CDKN2A/B, PTEN) and amplification of protooncogenes (EGFR, PDGFR).
WHO 2021 Classification incorporates molecular parameters in CNS tumor diagnosis.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · EPIDEMIOLOGY | MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | DIFFUSE GLIOMA

items.title: Classification and Molecular Characteristics of Diffuse Gliomas
items.content: The 2021 WHO Classification differentiates gliomas into 'adult type' or 'pediatric type' based on molecular genetic differences. Both types can be subclassified as high- or low-grade glioma, with further characterization by key molecular alterations. This classification aids in understanding prognosis and optimal therapy.
keyPoints: 2021 WHO Classification differentiates gliomas into 'adult type' and 'pediatric type'.
Subclassification based on histology: high-grade or low-grade glioma.
Molecular genetic differences are key to classification.
Inclusion of pediatric-type diffuse gliomas in adults due to molecular characteristics.
Improved understanding of prognosis and therapy through integrated diagnosis.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · EPIDEMIOLOGY | MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | DIFFUSE GLIOMA

items.title: Prognostic Impact of Molecular Alterations in Adult-Type Diffuse Gliomas
items.content: Adult-type diffuse gliomas are categorized based on IDH mutation status and 1p/19q co-deletion, which significantly influence prognosis. These molecular alterations define three groups: IDH-mutant astrocytoma (1p/19q intact), IDH-mutant oligodendroglioma (1p/19q co-deleted), and IDH wild-type glioblastoma.
keyPoints: IDH mutation status and 1p/19q co-deletion are critical for classification.
Prognosis varies significantly among the three groups: IDH-mutant astrocytoma, IDH-mutant oligodendroglioma, and IDH wild-type glioblastoma.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · ADULT-TYPE DIFFUSE GLIOMA (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for High-Grade IDH-Mutant Astrocytomas
items.content: High-grade IDH-mutant astrocytomas, including grade 3 and 4, are treated similarly to glioblastoma with maximal safe surgical resection followed by radiation therapy (RT) and adjuvant temozolomide. Median survival for high-grade anaplastic astrocytoma ranges from 3 to 9 years.
keyPoints: Treatment involves maximal safe resection, RT, and adjuvant temozolomide.
Median survival for high-grade anaplastic astrocytoma is 3 to 9 years.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · ADULT-TYPE DIFFUSE GLIOMA (part 1)

items.title: Management and Prognosis of Oligodendrogliomas
items.content: Oligodendrogliomas, characterized by IDH mutations and 1p/19q co-deletion, are responsive to therapy. Grade 2 oligodendrogliomas have a median survival exceeding 10 years, while grade 3 oligodendrogliomas also show good response with similar treatment strategies as high-grade astrocytomas.
keyPoints: Oligodendrogliomas have IDH mutations and 1p/19q co-deletion.
Grade 2 oligodendrogliomas have a median survival exceeding 10 years.
Treatment for grade 3 involves maximal resection, RT, and chemotherapy.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · ADULT-TYPE DIFFUSE GLIOMA (part 1)

items.title: Optimal Treatment Strategy for Glioblastoma
items.content: The standard treatment regimen for glioblastomas involves maximal surgical resection followed by involved-field external-beam radiation therapy (RT) and concomitant temozolomide, with adjuvant temozolomide extending over 6 months. This approach increases median survival to 15–18 months compared to RT alone.
keyPoints: Maximal surgical resection followed by involved-field external-beam RT (6000 cGy in thirty 200-cGy fractions) with concomitant temozolomide, then 6 months of adjuvant temozolomide.
Median survival with this regimen is increased to 15–18 months compared to only 12 months with RT alone.
5-year survival rate is approximately 10%.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · ADULT-TYPE DIFFUSE GLIOMA (part 2)

items.title: Prognostic Factors and Treatment of Recurrent Glioblastoma
items.content: For recurrent glioblastomas, treatment options include reoperation, reirradiation, and bevacizumab. Bevacizumab increases progression-free survival but not overall survival. Key adverse prognostic factors are older age, unmethylated MGMT promoter, poor Karnofsky performance status, and unresectable tumor.
keyPoints: Treatment options for recurrent glioblastoma include reoperation, reirradiation, and bevacizumab.
Bevacizumab increases progression-free survival but not overall survival; median overall survival in a phase 3 trial was approximately 10 months with similar toxicities compared to nivolumab.
Adverse prognostic factors include older age, unmethylated MGMT promoter, poor Karnofsky performance status, and unresectable tumor.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · ADULT-TYPE DIFFUSE GLIOMA (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Prognosis of Pediatric-Type Diffuse High-Grade Gliomas
items.content: Pediatric-type diffuse high-grade gliomas (HGGs) are distinct based on location and molecular alterations. They often lack IDH mutations, leading to previous misdiagnoses as glioblastoma. Two main types include H3K27-altered and H3G34-mutant gliomas.
keyPoints: Diffuse Midline Glioma, H3K27-Altered: Arise from midline structures; have K27M mutations in H3F3A gene; poor prognosis with median survival of about 1 year.
Diffuse Hemispheric Glioma, H3G34-Mutant: Occur in young adults; have H3.3-G34R/V mutations; treated similarly to GBM; median overall survival is 18–22 months.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · PEDIATRIC-TYPE DIFFUSE HIGH-GRADE GLIOMA | PEDIATRIC-TYPE LOW-GRADE GLIOMA

items.title: Management of Pediatric-Type Low-Grade Gliomas
items.content: Pediatric-type low-grade gliomas can occur in both pediatric and adult populations. They are often curable with gross total resection, especially when targetable mutations such as MYB or MAPK alterations (e.g., BRAF, FGFR) are present.
keyPoints: Occur in young to older adults; include MYB-altered or MAPK-altered gliomas.
Curative potential with gross total resection; systemic therapy effective with oral BRAF or MAPK inhibitors for targetable mutations.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · PEDIATRIC-TYPE DIFFUSE HIGH-GRADE GLIOMA | PEDIATRIC-TYPE LOW-GRADE GLIOMA

items.subtopic: CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA
items.title: Pilocytic Astrocytomas: Pediatric Considerations and BRAF Mutations
items.content: Pilocytic astrocytomas are the most common brain tumors in children, often found in the cerebellum. They typically present as cystic lesions with an enhancing mural nodule and have a favorable prognosis if completely resected. Many of these tumors harbor BRAF fusions or mutations.
keyPoints: Most common tumor of childhood
Typically occurs in the cerebellum
Often appears as cystic lesion with enhancing mural nodule
BRAF fusions or mutations frequently present
Potentially curable if completely resected
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA
items.title: Ependymomas: Location-Based Management and Recurrence
items.content: Ependymomas are classified by their location into supratentorial, posterior fossa, or spinal. Complete resection is potentially curative, while partial resection necessitates irradiation due to recurrence risk. Molecular stratification aids in management decisions.
keyPoints: Derived from ependymal cells
Classified by location: supratentorial, posterior fossa, spinal
Complete resection is potentially curative
Partially resected ependymomas require irradiation due to recurrence
Molecular stratification (e.g., ZFTA or YAP1 oncogenic fusion) guides management
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA
items.title: Primary CNS Lymphoma: Diagnosis and Management in Immunocompetent vs. Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: PCNSL is a rare brain tumor, more common in older immunocompetent individuals. It often presents as a mass lesion with neuropsychiatric symptoms. In immunocompromised patients, it tends to be more aggressive and associated with EBV. Diagnosis requires biopsy, and management includes imaging and CSF analysis.
keyPoints: <3% of primary brain tumors
Increasing incidence in older immunocompetent individuals
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in immunocompetent patients
In immunocompromised patients, often associated with EBV
Median age: 60 years for immunocompetent; 31 years for HIV-related
Diagnosis requires stereotactic biopsy and CSF analysis
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · CIRCUMSCRIBED ASTROCYTOMA AND OTHER GLIONEURONAL TUMORS | EPENDYMOMAS | PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma | PCNSL IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of PCNSL in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: PCNSL in immunocompromised patients often presents with multiple ring-enhancing lesions, making differentiation from metastases or infections like toxoplasmosis challenging. Diagnosis is typically confirmed through CSF examination for cytology and EBV DNA, along with brain PET imaging to assess lesion hypermetabolism. Brain biopsy may be necessary if diagnosis remains uncertain.
keyPoints: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions can mimic metastases or infections such as toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients.
Diagnosis involves CSF cytology and EBV DNA examination, toxoplasmosis serologic testing, brain PET imaging, and possibly brain biopsy.
High-dose methotrexate-based regimens are preferred for treatment; WBRT is reserved for those intolerant to systemic chemotherapy.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma | PCNSL IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.subtopic: Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma | PCNSL IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS
items.title: Treatment Strategies and Considerations in Immunocompromised Patients with PCNSL
items.content: In immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV or organ transplants, high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy is the treatment of choice. For HIV patients, initiating highly active antiretroviral therapy is crucial. In organ transplant recipients, reducing immunosuppression may improve outcomes.
keyPoints: High-dose methotrexate-based regimens are preferred for treating PCNSL in immunocompromised patients.
Initiation of highly active antiretroviral therapy is recommended for HIV-related PCNSL cases.
Reducing immunosuppression may improve outcomes in organ transplant recipients with PCNSL.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma | PCNSL IN IMMUNOCOMPROMISED PATIENTS

items.title: Medulloblastoma: Molecular Subgroups and Prognosis
items.content: Medulloblastomas are the most common malignant brain tumors in children, with four molecular subtypes affecting prognosis. WNT-activated subtype has the best outcome, while non-WNT/non-SHH group 3 has the worst due to disseminated CNS disease at diagnosis.
keyPoints: Most common malignant brain tumor of childhood (~20% of all primary CNS tumors in children).

Four molecular subgroups: WNT-activated (best outcome), SHH-activated, non-WNT/non-SHH group 3 (worst outcome with disseminated CNS disease at diagnosis), and non-WNT/non-SHH group 4 (30% have metastases at diagnosis but 5-year progression-free survival is 95%).
Approximately 70% of patients have long-term survival, often with significant neurocognitive impairment.
Treatment involves maximal surgical resection, craniospinal irradiation, and chemotherapy.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · MEDULLOBLASTOMA | PINEAL REGION TUMORS

items.title: Pineal Region Tumors: Presentation and Treatment
items.content: Tumors in the pineal region typically present with headache, visual symptoms, hydrocephalus, and Parinaud's syndrome. Pineocytomas and benign teratomas can be surgically resected, while germinomas respond to irradiation, and pineoblastomas require craniospinal RT and chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Tumors in the pineal region present with headache, visual symptoms, hydrocephalus, and Parinaud's syndrome (impaired upgaze and accommodation).
Pineocytomas and benign teratomas can be treated by surgical resection.
Germinomas respond to irradiation.
Pineoblastomas and nongerminomatous germ cell tumors require craniospinal RT and chemotherapy.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · MEDULLOBLASTOMA | PINEAL REGION TUMORS

items.title: Incidence and Presentation of Meningiomas
items.content: Meningiomas are the most common primary brain tumor, accounting for approximately 35% of all cases. They are more frequently diagnosed due to increased neuroimaging and have a higher incidence in older individuals, women, patients with NF2, and those with a history of cranial irradiation.
keyPoints: Most common primary brain tumor (~35%)
Incidence increases with age
More common in women and patients with NF2 or cranial irradiation history
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · MENINGIOMA

items.title: Management of Meningiomas Based on Size and Symptoms
items.content: Small, asymptomatic meningiomas can be observed with serial MRI studies. Larger, symptomatic meningiomas should be surgically resected if possible. Complete resection often cures the patient, while incomplete resections may require external-beam RT or SRS to manage recurrence.
keyPoints: Small, asymptomatic meningiomas: observe with serial MRI
Larger, symptomatic meningiomas: surgical resection recommended
Complete resection often cures; incomplete resections may recur
External-beam RT or SRS for recurrent or partially resected tumors
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · MENINGIOMA

items.subtopic: SCHWANNOMA | PITUITARY TUMORS | CRANIOPHARYNGIOMAS | OTHER BENIGN TUMORS
items.title: Management of Vestibular Schwannomas: When to Observe vs. Treat
items.content: Vestibular schwannomas, also known as acoustic neuromas, are benign tumors arising from the vestibular portion of the eighth cranial nerve and account for approximately 9% of primary brain tumors. They may be incidentally found on neuroimaging or present with symptoms such as unilateral hearing loss, dizziness, tinnitus, or brainstem compression. Small, asymptomatic lesions can often be managed with serial MRI monitoring, while larger lesions typically require surgical intervention or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS). The decision to treat is influenced by the tumor size, symptomatology, and patient preference, particularly concerning hearing preservation.
keyPoints: Vestibular schwannomas account for ~9% of primary brain tumors.
Common symptoms include unilateral hearing loss, dizziness, tinnitus, or brainstem compression.
Small asymptomatic lesions can be observed with serial MRIs.
Larger lesions should be treated with surgery or SRS.
Early surgical intervention in small vestibular schwannomas may preserve hearing.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · SCHWANNOMA | PITUITARY TUMORS | CRANIOPHARYNGIOMAS | OTHER BENIGN TUMORS

items.subtopic: SCHWANNOMA | PITUITARY TUMORS | CRANIOPHARYNGIOMAS | OTHER BENIGN TUMORS
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Craniopharyngiomas
items.content: Craniopharyngiomas are rare, benign tumors that arise from remnants of Rathke's pouch. They have a bimodal age distribution, affecting children and adults between 55-65 years. These tumors present with headaches, visual impairment, impaired growth in children, and hypopituitarism in adults. Treatment typically involves surgery or radiation therapy (RT), sometimes combined. The papillary subtype of craniopharyngiomas often harbors BRAF V600E mutations, making them amenable to treatment with RAF/MEK inhibitors.
keyPoints: Craniopharyngiomas are partially calcified, solid or mixed solid-cystic tumors.
They have a bimodal distribution: predominantly in children and adults aged 55-65 years.
Presentations include headaches, visual impairment, impaired growth in children, and hypopituitarism in adults.
Treatment involves surgery, RT, or both.
Papillary subtype often has BRAF V600E mutations treatable with RAF/MEK inhibitors.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · SCHWANNOMA | PITUITARY TUMORS | CRANIOPHARYNGIOMAS | OTHER BENIGN TUMORS

items.subtopic: NEUROCUTANEOUS SYNDROMES (PHAKOMATOSES) | NEUROFIBROMATOSIS TYPE 1 (von RECKLINGHAUSEN'S DISEASE)
items.title: Understanding Neurofibromatosis Type 1: Incidence, Genetic Basis, and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1) is an autosomal dominant disorder with variable penetrance, affecting approximately 1 in 2600–3000 individuals. It arises from mutations in the NF1 gene on chromosome 17q11.2, which encodes neurofibromin, a negative regulator of the RAS-MAP kinase pathway. Clinical manifestations include multiple cutaneous neurofibromas, café-au-lait spots, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, and an increased risk for various nervous system tumors.
keyPoints: NF1 incidence: ~1 in 2600–3000
Autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance
NF1 gene location: chromosome 17q11.2
Neurofibromin function: negative regulator of RAS-MAP kinase pathway
Clinical manifestations: neurofibromas, café-au-lait spots, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, nervous system tumors
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROCUTANEOUS SYNDROMES (PHAKOMATOSES) | NEUROFIBROMATOSIS TYPE 1 (von RECKLINGHAUSEN'S DISEASE) | NEUROFIBROMATOSIS TYPE 2

items.title: Key Features of Neurofibromatosis Type 2: Incidence, Genetic Basis, and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2) is an autosomal dominant disorder with full penetrance, occurring in about 1 in 25,000–40,000 individuals. It results from mutations in the NF2 gene on chromosome 22q, encoding merlin, a tumor suppressor protein. The hallmark of NF2 is bilateral vestibular schwannomas (>90% of patients), along with multiple meningiomas and spinal ependymomas or astrocytomas.
keyPoints: NF2 incidence: 1 in 25,000–40,000
Autosomal dominant inheritance with full penetrance
NF2 gene location: chromosome 22q
Merlin function: tumor suppressor protein
Clinical manifestations: bilateral vestibular schwannomas (>90%), multiple meningiomas, spinal ependymomas/astrocytomas

items.subtopic: TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS (BOURNEVILLE DISEASE) | TUMORS METASTATIC TO THE BRAIN
items.title: Understanding Tuberous Sclerosis: Genetic Basis and Clinical Management
items.content: Tuberous sclerosis is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the TSC1 or TSC2 genes, leading to dysregulation of mTOR signaling. This results in a range of clinical manifestations including seizures, intellectual disability, and various skin lesions. Management often involves anticonvulsants for seizure control, while SEGs can be effectively treated with mTOR inhibitors like sirolimus or everolimus.
keyPoints: Incidence: ~1 in 5000–10,000 live births
Genetic cause: Mutations in TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin)
Clinical features: Seizures, intellectual disability, adenoma sebaceum, shagreen patch, hypomelanotic macules, periwugal fibromas, renal angiomyolipomas, cardiac rhabdomyomas
Increased incidence of subependymal nodules, cortical tubers, and SEGs
Management: Anticonvulsants for seizures; mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus or everolimus) for SEGs
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS (BOURNEVILLE DISEASE) | TUMORS METASTATIC TO THE BRAIN

items.subtopic: TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS (BOURNEVILLE DISEASE) | TUMORS METASTATIC TO THE BRAIN
items.title: Patterns and Management of Brain Metastases
items.content: Brain metastases typically arise from hematogenous spread, with lung and breast carcinomas being the most common sources. They predominantly occur supratentorially (85%) due to blood flow distribution. Melanoma has a high propensity for brain metastasis (~80% at autopsy). Management includes addressing leptomeningeal metastases, which are frequent in hematologic malignancies and certain solid tumors like breast and lung cancers.
keyPoints: Common sources: Lung (primary), breast carcinomas
Distribution: ~85% supratentorial; 15% posterior fossa
Melanoma propensity: Found in 80% of patients at autopsy
Leptomeningeal metastases: Common from hematologic malignancies, breast and lung cancers
Spinal cord compression: Primarily from prostate and breast cancer
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS (BOURNEVILLE DISEASE) | TUMORS METASTATIC TO THE BRAIN

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF METASTASES | DEFINITIVE TREATMENT | RADIATION THERAPY
items.title: MRI Visualization of Brain Metastases: Characteristics and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Brain metastases are typically visualized on MRI as well-circumscribed lesions with variable perilesional edema. Enhancement patterns can be ring-like or diffuse, and hemorrhage is more common in lung cancer metastases due to its prevalence among brain metastases. While imaging often suffices for diagnosis, biopsy may be necessary if systemic cancer presents without an accessible site for sampling.
keyPoints: MRI shows well-circumscribed lesions with variable edema
Enhancement can be ring or diffuse; hemorrhage common in lung cancer metastases
Biopsy rarely needed but required in ~10% of cases when systemic biopsy is inaccessible
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · DIAGNOSIS OF METASTASES | DEFINITIVE TREATMENT | RADIATION THERAPY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF METASTASES | DEFINITIVE TREATMENT | RADIATION THERAPY
items.title: Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases: WBRT vs. SRS
items.content: Whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT) traditionally involves 3000 cGy in 10 fractions, providing rapid palliation but limited survival benefit and potential neurocognitive toxicity. Hippocampal avoidance can mitigate cognitive decline. Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS), now preferred for its precision, treats lesions effectively with high local control rates (80-90%) and is suitable for up to 10 metastases ≤3 cm in size, often leading to cure.
keyPoints: WBRT: 3000 cGy over 10 fractions; ~80% improve with glucocorticoids
SRS preferred for precision; effective for lesions ≤3 cm and up to 10 lesions
Hippocampal avoidance during WBRT preserves cognitive function
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · DIAGNOSIS OF METASTASES | DEFINITIVE TREATMENT | RADIATION THERAPY

items.title: Benefits and Timing of Surgical Intervention for Brain Metastases
items.content: Surgical extirpation of a single brain metastasis followed by whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) is superior to WBRT alone in improving disease control. Surgery can rapidly ameliorate symptoms, improve edema control, and prolong survival, particularly in patients with highly radioresistant lesions like renal carcinoma. Postoperative focal radiation or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) may be used if there's concern about residual tumor, but postoperative WBRT is often avoided due to cognitive effects.
keyPoints: Surgical extirpation of a single brain metastasis followed by WBRT improves disease control over WBRT alone.
Surgical resection can rapidly improve symptoms and edema control, and prolong survival.
Postoperative focal RT or SRS may be used if residual tumor is suspected; avoid postoperative WBRT due to cognitive effects.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · SURGERY | CHEMOTHERAPY | LEPTOMENINGEAL METASTASES

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Leptomeningeal Metastases
items.content: Leptomeningeal metastases, also known as carcinomatous meningitis or meningeal carcinomatosis, occur when tumor cells reach the subarachnoid space. They are most common in acute leukemias among hematologic malignancies and breast, lung, and melanoma cancers among solid tumors. These metastases can arise from arterial circulation or retrograde venous flow and may develop following brain metastasis resection, occurring in almost 40% of patients with cerebellar metastasis removal.
keyPoints: Leptomeningeal metastases are also known as carcinomatous meningitis or meningeal carcinomatosis.
Common among acute leukemias and aggressive lymphomas; breast, lung, and melanoma cancers in solid tumors.
Tumor cells reach the subarachnoid space via arterial circulation or retrograde venous flow.
Leptomeningeal metastases may develop post brain metastasis resection, occurring in ~40% of patients with cerebellar metastasis removal.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · SURGERY | CHEMOTHERAPY | LEPTOMENINGEAL METASTASES

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY AND IMAGING DIAGNOSIS | Leptomeningeal Metastases
items.title: Recognizing Multilevel Symptoms and Signs in Leptomeningeal Metastases
items.content: Leptomeningeal metastases present with a variety of symptoms along the neuraxis, including radiculopathies, cranial neuropathies, seizures, confusion, and encephalopathy. These symptoms are due to tumor spread within the subarachnoid space.
keyPoints: Multilevel symptoms include lumbar and cervical radiculopathies, cranial neuropathies, seizures, confusion, and encephalopathy.
Focal deficits like hemiparesis or aphasia are rare unless there is direct brain infiltration.
New-onset limb pain in patients with breast cancer, lung cancer, or melanoma should raise suspicion for leptomeningeal metastases.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY AND IMAGING DIAGNOSIS | Leptomeningeal Metastases

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY AND IMAGING DIAGNOSIS | Leptomeningeal Metastases
items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Approaches in Leptomeningeal Metastases
items.content: Diagnosing leptomeningeal metastases is challenging due to the elusive nature of tumor cells. MRI can be definitive, but CSF cytologic examination remains the gold standard despite its limitations.
keyPoints: MRI is diagnostic in ~75% of patients and more often positive in those with solid tumors.
CSF cytologic examination is positive in only 50% on the first lumbar puncture and misses 10% after three samples.
Elevated protein concentration, elevated white blood cell count, and hypoglycorrhachia (<25%) are CSF abnormalities associated with leptomeningeal metastases.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · CLINICAL FEATURES | LABORATORY AND IMAGING DIAGNOSIS | Leptomeningeal Metastases

items.subtopic: EPIDURAL METASTASIS | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Metastasis
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Approach for Epidural Metastasis
items.content: Epidural metastasis, occurring in 3–5% of patients with systemic malignancy, typically presents with back pain that precedes neurologic symptoms. The thoracic spine is most commonly affected. Diagnosis relies on MRI to delineate epidural tumors, essential for any cancer patient with severe back pain.
keyPoints: Epidural metastasis occurs in 3–5% of patients with systemic malignancy.
Back pain precedes neurologic symptoms and is exacerbated by lying down.
~50% of patients present with leg weakness; ~25% have sphincter problems at diagnosis.
MRI is the preferred diagnostic tool for epidural metastasis.
Differential diagnoses include epidural abscess, hematomas, lipomatosis, and extramedullary hematopoiesis.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · EPIDURAL METASTASIS | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Metastasis

items.subtopic: EPIDURAL METASTASIS | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Metastasis
items.title: Treatment Strategies for Epidural Metastasis
items.content: Immediate treatment of epidural metastasis is crucial. Surgical resection followed by radiation therapy (RT) is superior to RT alone, provided the patient can tolerate surgery. For those unable to undergo surgery or with radiosensitive tumors like lymphoma, RT is primary. Stereotactic radiotherapy is used for resistant tumors or reirradiation.
keyPoints: Immediate treatment of epidural metastasis is necessary.
Surgical resection followed by RT is superior to RT alone if the patient can tolerate surgery.
RT is the mainstay for patients unable to undergo surgery or with radiosensitive tumors.
Stereotactic radiotherapy is used for resistant tumors or reirradiation.
Chemotherapy is rarely used unless there's minimal neurologic deficit and a highly chemosensitive tumor.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · EPIDURAL METASTASIS | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Metastasis

items.title: Acute Toxicity Management with Glucocorticoids
items.content: Acute cerebral toxicity following RT can disrupt the blood-brain barrier, leading to edema and elevated intracranial pressure. Symptoms include headache, lethargy, nausea, and vomiting.
keyPoints: Occurs within days of RT
Symptoms: headache, lethargy, nausea, vomiting
Prevention and treatment with glucocorticoids
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROLOGIC TOXICITY OF THERAPY

items.title: Understanding Pseudoprogression in Malignant Glioma
items.content: Early delayed toxicity, or pseudoprogression, mimics tumor recurrence on MRI but represents inflammation and necrotic debris. It is more frequent with concurrent chemotherapy like temozolomide.
keyPoints: Occurs weeks to months after RT
Mimics tumor recurrence on imaging
Frequently associated with concurrent chemotherapy (e.g., temozolomide)
May resolve spontaneously or require glucocorticoids, resection, or bevacizumab
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROLOGIC TOXICITY OF THERAPY

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Late Delayed Toxicity
items.content: Late delayed toxicities are often irreversible and include radiation necrosis and leukoencephalopathy. Radiation necrosis appears months to years post-RT, is symptomatic, and may require resection or bevacizumab.
keyPoints: Most serious due to irreversibility
Radiation necrosis: focal mass, contrast enhancing, symptomatic (e.g., seizures)
Leukoencephalopathy: cognitive impairment, gait disorder, urinary incontinence
Risk factors: increased age for leukoencephalopathy; predisposition for necrosis
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · NEUROLOGIC TOXICITY OF THERAPY

items.title: Understanding Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: Chemotherapy can lead to peripheral neuropathy, which varies by agent. Vincristine causes paresthesias and motor dysfunction with autonomic impairment, while cisplatin leads to large-fiber sensory loss resulting in sensory ataxia.
keyPoints: Neurotoxicity is second only to myelosuppression as a dose-limiting toxicity of chemotherapeutic agents.
Vincristine causes paresthesias with motor dysfunction and autonomic impairment, including ileus.
Cisplatin results in large-fiber sensory loss causing sensory ataxia without cutaneous sensory loss or weakness.
items.reference: Primary and Metastatic Tumors of the Nervous System · TOXICITY FROM CHEMOTHERAPY

items.title: Recognizing Encephalopathy and Seizures from Chemotherapy
items.content: Encephalopathy and seizures are common toxicities. Ifosfamide causes reversible encephalopathy, while fludarabine can lead to permanent global encephalopathy. Bevacizumab may cause posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome.
keyPoints: Encephalopathy and seizures are frequent toxicities from chemotherapeutic drugs.
Ifosfamide causes severe but reversible encephalopathy upon drug discontinuation.
Fludarabine can lead to a severe, potentially permanent global encephalopathy.
Bevacizumab is associated with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome.
